sentences.text: Very briefly, the Ukrainian community is primarily consisting of peasants.
There are several monastic orders, like the Brazilians, Redemptorist, and so forth.
But these orders accepted only educated Ukrainians.
The Metropolitan Sheptytskyi decided to create a framework for sons of peasants without education.
And this was the order, which was created at the turn of the century and dedicated itself to reviving Eastern monasticism.
Without going into too many details, the composition of the monks' body was primarily people of the land.
Some were educated.
Some were illiterate.
Some had skills.
Some had to be taught skills.
There were some who are quite well-educated.
There was an art expert and painter of icons.
There was a man who was married, and he was an accountant in the town.
In an epidemic, his wife and children died, so he joined the monastery, but a highly educated man.
Marek was a former banker.
So it was a mixture.
Yes.
Yes.
The same background.
Some were.
They didn't like Jews.
Yes.
Yes.
No.
No.
You see, the fact that they liked or disliked Jews had nothing to do with what they have done.
They resented the fact the Jews were being killed.
They resented the bestiality, and they tried to help.
Because they felt that it was in their limited circumstances, they couldn't, in all their conscience, to sit quiet on the sidelines.
Some objected to having Jews in the monastery.
Quite openly.
Said so.
They said that the community is being endangered.
But they never betrayed the Jew.
Never, you see, interfered with it.
But they are entitled to their views.
Very little.
Very little.

Well, first of all, there was a Ukrainian paper, which was controlled by the Nazis and by Ukrainian nationalists, pro-Nazi nationalists, which provided the usual information issued by the ministerium, minister of propaganda.
In the library in the city, there were German newspapers.
But it was dangerous for me to show that I speak German, so I kept away from it.
Radio was the same.
There was some local radio, but most of the news came through hearsay and visitors.
It was arranged for me to go and see him after I adapted to the environment.
And a mother superior, over the Brazilian nuns, took me by train at night-- which was very dangerous because she endangered her own life.
Her name was Monika Polanska.
Monika Polanska was a physician.
She was born in Philadelphia, a Ukrainian, and she was widowed.
And she sold her property and went back and became a nun and a superior of this particular house in Podmichalowce, was the name.
So she took me there, transported me with her.
And the chaplain of this monastery, Father Kiprion, kept my brother.
Because he also was being trained to be a Ukrainian, as you said.
In order to put my mind at ease, I was there for a week.
And then when Mother Monika Polanska had to see a lawyer in a nearby town, she took me there.
And we traveled by sleigh in the winter.
And from there, I went to another monastery.
Then, I didn't see my brother for a year, until I came back to Lwow.
And then he was brought to Lwow too, as the Germans were approaching.
No, he took care of pigs.
No.
No.
Certainly not.
As a boy, as an orphan.
I was in the monastery working in the archives as a monk.
In Lwow, and the monks, who ran the household and the stables were living in the same monastery where I did.
And my brother used to go and eat there.
So I have seen him daily.
But we never had any contact-- unless under special circumstances.
Oh, we recognized, but we tried not to--
--acknowledge each other because we both understood that it is better that people don't know that we are brothers.
But he used to come and visit me in my cell.
Cell is the room where I lived.
One, two, three, four.
Yes, indirectly.
I knew that she was left in the ghetto.
And while I was in the first one, I went with Father Marek to visit her, which was taking our lives in our hands.
And I went to see her.
And I told her that she should make an effort to get out.
She told me, I'm a widow.
My husband is dead.
Oh, surely.
My youngest son is not normal under the circumstances.
He will not be able to survive.
I can not leave him.
So here I am.
And besides, I'm not so sure of that with all my world gone-- because it was quite evident, at that time-- that I wished to survive.
Subsequently, my impression is some efforts were made to get her out.
It's not possible from the ghetto.
But Father  Marek once brought me a little note from her.
Written to me-- 'I'm still alive.' I translate. 'Liam [SOBBING] is no longer with us.
Forget us and enjoy life.
Signed [NON-ENGLISH].
End of story.
That was the last communication.
Slowly.
You see, first of all, Stalingrad.
Don't forget that the Ukrainians are communicating among themselves and the police and so forth.
It was quite clear that trains full of wounded and frozen people are coming back from Russia.
Secondly, there was a partisan activity.
Ukrainians created a partisan force, which, for the Germans, because the Germans were beastly towards the Ukrainians too-- in a different way.
And thirdly, we learned about Stalingrad, probably, two or three months after it happened.
Finally, a partisan division, Kopac, marched through the back of the German front, from Belorussia, all the way into Hungary.
And passed near us, where I was, in the mountains.
Kopac-- that is the name of the general.
Yes.
No, it was a semi-regular group.
It was a division, which crossed through the forest, the German line, and marched all the way from Belorussia, cutting supply lines of the Germans during the major battles.
It's quite notorious now.
No, they was Red Army.
But some Ukrainians joined.
There were some Jews who joined.
We knew that something is happening.
Also, we knew that the Germans is heavily bombed.
Hearsay, Peters was a German.
Hmm?

Well, as the fronts started to approach, as [NON-ENGLISH] Klyment, the brother of the metropolitan, who was in charge of all that, realized that things are changing, he started to collect the Jews in Lwow.
In Lwow, in the main city.
So my brother was brought from a village where he was to Lwow and started to take care of pigs in the residence.
It was a huge compound.
I was brought back to Lwow.
And some other children and some other Jews, I discovered, were brought to Lwow.
No.
--we are told.
You are going here, going there.
The same way as a monk.
Now, then the Germans-- the Russians started to bomb the city, night after night.
Subsequently, you could hear the artillery battles.
And then there was quiet for two, three months, so the Germans running out in the less and less orderly fashion.
One day, carrying books to the residence of the metropolitan, I stumbled on the Russian sniper, a woman, lying there, and I realized that the Russians are in the city.
What does it mean?
Formal?
No, not only that, but I kept still my Ukrainian identity.
I didn't want to say anything.
I still acted and waited for three, four weeks until you see the situation stabilize.
The Germans could have come back.
I'm sorry.
No, not at that time.
I discussed it with Klyment, who was a very wise man.
And he pointed out to me that if I stay in Lwow, I would be mobilized to the Russian army.
And as Poland is liberated, and there is a Polish committee of national liberation in Lublin.

It would be prudent to move there and, contact [INAUDIBLE] because he had the same misconceptions I had, that Jews would take care of these survivors, and they will be, in a way, provided with assistance and so forth, which was not the case.
Anyway, after about two months, I decided to move on.
And I crossed the border, or the demarcation line is better, between the newly created Poland and Russia.
Because the territory where I was was formally annexed to the Soviet Union.
In Rawa Ruska.
Yes.
This was the border line, is after Rawa Ruska.
Still today.
First, to Rawa Ruska.
Then I crossed the distance of the local Greek Catholic priests.
Until Rawa Ruska, yes.
From Rawa Ruska, no.
It's sort of a black cassock with a leather belt, which a novice wears.
I had normal clothing because this was used only during services in church, but not in work.
Normal clothes, pants, shirt.
I had a little lambswool sort of a-- I looked like dressed like a peasant.
I assume that it must have been September 1900-- first week of September 1944.
The Soviets liberated the city in June 1944.
I stayed through July and most of August in the city, in the monastery.
The decision for me to leave was made by Klyment, the brother of the metropolitan.
Metropolitan was very ill.
He died three months later.
Secondly, Klyment told me, before you leave Poland, finish your education because, otherwise, you're can not to go to a university.
Remember, that you have to finish your university studies.
And if you get out after the war, go to Rome and report what you have seen.
When I have provided him with the decision that I intend to go-- so in the monastery, it was arranged, first of all, to provide me with shirts.
In those days, it was a major effort to make sure that I have a decent pair of shoes, socks, clothing.
They looked into their chest to find some money and gave me what they had.
Gave me a bottle of wine, some bread.
And the night before I left, the monks, after services, came to my cell one by one, saying goodbye.
And everybody brought a little gift.
Whatever they had, which means a picture-- the holy pictures, which they have-- a prayer book, a pair of socks, a handkerchief.
It was like a family sending a member of the family away.
Then I was asked to come to the residence of the metropolitan.
He was on his deathbed.
And I was brought there.
And he greeted me and blessed me, the same, his brother.
The next morning, I went on the road, caught a Soviet truck.
And they transported me to Rawa Ruska.
End of story.
In Rawa Ruska.
Of the monastery?
More or less, the same when I left my mother.
Because I dealt with people.
I lived with people, who have taken extraordinary risks, provided me with not only shelter, but with an environment where the terrific emotional shocks and wounds could heal, somewhat.
And therefore, I felt sorry to leave them.
But I realized, it's not my environment and I have to.
You asked about incidents, close shaves.
Well, I was working in Lwow, maybe eight months before the Germans left, with a monk by the name Varlaam.
If you listen, it's a old Slavonic name.
Varlaam sounds like Abraham.
And this monk had round, sad Jewish eyes.
He was bald.
It was a beard.
He was a Ukrainian.
We were stopped by a German patrol and Ukrainian patrol, and they turned to me and said, Reverend, Brother, we think that your colleague is a Jew.
They took him into a doorway to take his pants off.
I was standing there.
I didn't get gray, but the five or 10 minutes before they came out, apologizing, it was a rather unusual experience.
I had many shaves like that.
But it's too long, and it would take too many tapes.
And I think it's repetitious.
Best moment.
I used to get depressed from time to time.
But I didn't have, yet, the benefit of a psychobabble and mental health clinics.
So I can only see it today.
There's a monk.
He's still in Canada, Brother Lawrence, he was an a purist.
So he used to go the superior and say, I think that Roman has to help me today in the apiary because you want to extract honey.
When I used to come there, he used to cut, you see, a honeycomb out of there-- off their beehive and give it to me, and so I could enjoy some honey, which did a great deal for my soul.
And my depression was dissipated.
Hm?
When I went to the mother house, and they didn't know that I'm Jewish, and they assumed that I'm a novice.
So they exposed me to the training of a novice.
And I had to sit on my knees for two, three hours and listen to singing-- which was totally alien-- and participate in all kinds of things.
I thought I would go mad.
Until I started to adapt to it.
Second point, I was perpetually hungry.
Because food was brought and served-- there was a huge table in the refectory.
There was roughly 80 monks.
And first, the abbot used to get-- was served, and then down the road.
But the novices were at the end.
So usually, the abbot used to knock, it's time to go back to prayers and to work.
So when food came to the novices, we had no time to eat.
It's part and parcel of the training.
Yes.
Across the border, actually, singing in the funeral of a peasant because you wouldn't cross the border at the demarcation line easily.
So a peasant died in the village, but the cemetery was in Soviet Union, but he lived in Poland.
So I went with the priest from Rawa Ruska to participate in the funeral.
And I sung at-- as a cantor, which I'm trained, by the way, with the funeral.
But I returned with the priest from the Polish end.
And that's the way I crossed the border.
And from then on, I tried to become myself again.
Kurt Lewin.
Yes.
For only three weeks.
Then I realized that there are pogroms, Jews are being killed.
And although the Germans are gone, the Poles are still creating a danger.
So I shifted to a Polish name and that's it.
Roman Matkowski.
I fabricated them.
Yes.
I had to think like Roman Mytka.
Otherwise, I wouldn't survive or I would go mad.
Equally so, I served in the Polish army as Roman Matkowski, so I had to think like Roman Matkowski.
I had to suppress and just put things in a little corner.
I was an infantry man.
And I was a second lieutenant because I knew how to read and write.
And then after the war, for a short while, I was sort of a head of a office, you know, clerk, senior clerk.
But you see, the same-- the question which you ask is the same, which faces me today.
In order to communicate, with my present environment, I cannot revert either to my life as a monk, or my life in the ghetto, or life in the Polish army, or, for that matter, life in this era.
So this is a corner, you see which--
Quite a number of people because I make friends very easily, and some survivors.
No.
From September or October 1944 until May, June, until August 1945.
No, I demobilized because, you see, having served during the war in combat, I was entitled to be demobilized immediately when the war ended or shortly afterwards.
I picked up two Catholic priests Father Marek and another one, and we ran away from Poland to Germany.
No.
No, but I was stationed near Warsaw.

And there was a gymnasium, and I went to this fine lady, who was a director of the gymnasium, told her that I am a Pole from Lwow, and I couldn't finish my schooling, and I would like to get a degree, and how to go about it.
Well, she said, you can sit down to an examination as an external student.
We will test you and see whether you can do it.
So I was there for almost two months.
And the teachers were very kind because they worked with me on what I do, subjects, prepared me for the examination.
And I sit down, and I passed it, and I have a certificate, which is a baccalaureate.
And your certificate is as Roman Matkowski?
Yes, Roman Matkowski.
Yes.
No, I just watched it.
No, I didn't function in Poland as a Jew.
I smuggled these two priests out of Poland illegally to Czechoslovakia and from there to Germany.
Yes.
We traveled.
I described it in the book.
Yes, certainly, because we could be caught by the Russians.
And once we crossed the Russian demarcation line, there was no risk anymore.
I would assume today looking, I didn't know, precisely, but I think that there must have been some underground organization guided by the Haganah people in Europe, which was called Bricha.
And they organized the transports of Jews-- men, women, and children-- westward from Poland to Germany.
I forged Jewish papers for them.
I became Kurt Lewin the minute I left the Polish border.
There was no need to do it.
That's a complicated question.
I had on several occasions some problems to prove my identity.
First, get out of Poland because I understood being among the Poles and living among these people as a Pole, there's no place for a Jew there.
So my choice was either to submerge and remain as a Pole, which I could easily.
Because I could also make a career if I wanted to because of reasons, which, again, I described in my book, so I don't want to belabor this.
But I had no interest in it.
So I tried to move westward.
And having been exposed to the Holocaust and a variety of other things, I intended to go what was [INAUDIBLE] Israel and Palestine and start life there.
Especially that I came out of an environment, which was Zionist.
Yes.
I returned to be an Orthodox Jew.
I joined a group of Orthodox Jews in Italy and traveled with them to Palestine.
And while I was at the Hebrew University, I was a practicing Orthodox Jew, yes.
Not at all.
Immediately.
No problem.
Very few and mostly unsatisfactory.
I arrived in Rome and went to join AJDC, American Jewish Distribution Committee, headed by, at that time, a very good-looking lady, whom I know socially here.
She's in New York.
I said, here I came from Poland, and I don't know anybody.
Would you guide me, so she started to yell at me, and told me to get out.
Now, it's lunch time.
How many tapes do you want still?
